# Special Issue on Machine Learning in Antenna Design, Modeling, and Measurements

**JULY 2022 | VOLUME 70 | NUMBER 7**

![Special Issue on Smart Electromagnetic Environment Image](/images/aps-images/ieee_tap/Machine_Learning_in_Antenna_Design_Modeling_and_Measurements.jpg)

![Special Issue on Smart Electromagnetic Environment Image](/images/aps-images/ieee_tap/Machine_Learning_in_Antenna_Design_Modeling_and_Measurements.jpg)

*This Special Issue highlights several promising avenues of ongoing research to integrate ML and data-driven AI techniques in the field of antennas and propagation. It aims to provide a broader perspective, outlining recent successes, opportunities, and open challenges for ML focusing on applications to antennas, radar, scattering, and propagation problems. The 25 featured articles cover a wide range of AI-empowered methods for antenna design, antenna arrays, metasurfaces, CEM, and other related applications.*

SUMMARY:
The surrogate model operates within a reduced-dimensionality domain, guided by directions corresponding to maximum antenna response variability identified through fast global sensitivity analysis, tailored explicitly for domain determination. Extensive comparative experiments involving various planar antennas demonstrate the competitive operation of the presented technique over machine learning algorithms operating in full-dimensionality space and direct EM-driven bio-inspired optimization techniques. We extensively validate our technique using several microstrip antennas and compare it to direct EM-driven nature-inspired optimization, multiple start gradient search, and a machine learning framework operating in full-dimensionality space. The second best method is Algorithm III (machine learning working in the original design variable space X), for which the design quality is essentially the same as for the proposed approach for Antennas I and IV.

# Antenna optimization using machine learning with reduced-dimensionality surrogates

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 14**, Article number: 21567 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

3661 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-024-72478-w/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

In modern times, antenna design has become more demanding than ever. The escalating requirements for performance and functionality drive the development of intricately structured antennas, where parameters must be meticulously adjusted to achieve peak performance. Often, global adjustments to geometry are necessary for optimal results. However, direct manipulation of antenna responses evaluated with full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulation models using conventional nature-inspired methods entails significant computational costs. Alternatively, surrogate-based techniques show promise but are impeded by dimensionality-related challenges and nonlinearity of antenna outputs. This study introduces an innovative technique for swiftly optimizing antennas. It leverages a machine learning framework with an infill criterion employing predicted enhancement of the merit function, utilizing a particle swarm optimizer as the primary search engine, and employs kriging for constructing the underlying surrogate model. The surrogate model operates within a reduced-dimensionality domain, guided by directions corresponding to maximum antenna response variability identified through fast global sensitivity analysis, tailored explicitly for domain determination. Operating within this reduced domain enables building dependable metamodels at a significantly lower computational cost. To address accuracy loss resulting from dimensionality reduction, the global optimization phase is supplemented by local sensitivity-based parameter adjustment. Extensive comparative experiments involving various planar antennas demonstrate the competitive operation of the presented technique over machine learning algorithms operating in full-dimensionality space and direct EM-driven bio-inspired optimization techniques.

# Antenna Design and Optimization Using Machine Learning

## On-Demand Short Course

Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. As the complexity of antennas increases each day, antenna designers can take advantage of machine learning to generate trained models for their physical antenna designs and perform fast and intelligent optimization on these trained models. Using the trained models, different optimization algorithms and goals can be run quickly, in seconds, that can be utilized for comparison studies, stochastic analysis for tolerance studies etc.

This short course presents the process of fast and intelligent optimization by adopting the Design of Experiments (DOE) and Machine Learning using Altair FEKO. We discuss specific examples that showcase the advantages of using ML for antenna design and optimization.

Abstract
--------

Recently, there has been increasing interest in integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques into the optimization and measurement of antenna designs, driven by their potential to improve both design efficiency and performance. This review examines the latest advancements in applying AI/ML approaches to antenna design optimization. It explores the use of various AI/ML algorithms such as neural networks, decision trees, genetic algorithms, and particle swarm optimization in this context. The paper also highlights the challenges and limitations associated with employing AI/ML in antenna design, optimization, and measurement, while identifying future research opportunities in the field. The review concludes that AI/ML approaches have the capacity to transform antenna design by offering quicker and more precise solutions to complex problems. However, since the field is still in its early stages, continuous research and development are necessary to address these challenges and fully capitalize on the capabilities of AI/ML in optimizing antenna design.

The antenna systems are essential to modern wireless communication, as they use multiple transmitting and receiving antennas to enhance data throughput, reliability, and signal quality without needing additional bandwidth [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR11 "Zaidi AM, Singh SP. A dual-band rat-race coupler for high band ratio wireless applications. IEEE Trans Instrum Meas. 2021. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIM.2021.3119131
                  
                ."),[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR12 "Al-Eryani Y, Akrout M, Hossain E. Antenna clustering for simultaneous wireless information and power transfer in a MIMO full-duplex system: a deep reinforcement learning-based design. IEEE Trans Commun. 2021;69(4):2331–45."),[13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR13 "Tuan PV, Son PN. Intelligent reflecting surface assisted transceiver design optimization in non-linear SWIPT network with heterogeneous users. Wirel Netw. 2022;28(5):1889–908.")]. Antenna achieves these gains by transmitting independent data streams across different antennas, increasing the system’s overall capacity. However, designing antennas that perform optimally across diverse, dynamic environments presents several complex challenges. A primary difficulty in antenna design is managing multiple, often conflicting performance parameters, such as gain, bandwidth, radiation pattern, and impedance matching. Unlike single-antenna systems, antenna designs must ensure that each antenna element’s characteristics harmonize with the others to prevent issues like mutual coupling, which can degrade signal quality [[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR12 "Al-Eryani Y, Akrout M, Hossain E. Antenna clustering for simultaneous wireless information and power transfer in a MIMO full-duplex system: a deep reinforcement learning-based design. IEEE Trans Commun. 2021;69(4):2331–45."), [14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR14 "Zhang B, Jin C, Cao K, Lv Q, Mittra R. Cognitive conformal antenna array exploiting deep reinforcement learning method. IEEE Trans Antennas Propag. 2021;70(7):5094–104."), [15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR15 "Zhang X, Zhang X, Wang L. Antenna design by an adaptive variable differential artificial bee colony algorithm. IEEE Trans Magn. 2017;54(3):1–4.")]. Furthermore, antennas require high isolation between elements to minimize interference, which becomes increasingly difficult as the number of antennas and their density increase. To address these design requirements, engineers traditionally rely on empirical methods and CEM simulations, which, though accurate, are often time-consuming and computationally intensive. As antenna systems become more complex—such as in large-scale antenna configurations used in 5G networks [[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR16 "Pavithran S, Viswasom S, Asha J et al. Designing of a 5G multiband antenna using decision tree and random forest regression models, In: 2021 8th international conference on signal processing and integrated networks (SPIN), 2021, pp. 626–631.")], where hundreds of antennas may be deployed—traditional methods struggle to scale efficiently.

AI techniques such as deep learning, reinforcement learning, and genetic algorithms can utilize historical design data to make predictions or perform optimizations. These techniques can automate the designs & measurement process, lowering the time required for manual optimization. AI and ML are rapidly advancing fields with the potential to transform numerous industries, including antenna design optimization. The application of AI techniques in antenna designs & measurement has the potential to increase the efficiency, accuracy and speed of the design process. With AI/ML algorithms, it is possible to optimize antenna parameters, reduce the time and effort required in the design process, and improve the overall performance of antennas.

AI/ML algorithms are well-suited for antenna design as they efficiently navigate high-dimensional parameter spaces, optimizing various physical and electrical attributes simultaneously. Unlike conventional methods, AI/ML approaches handle non-linear relationships between input parameters (e.g., antenna size, shape, and material) and performance metrics (e.g., gain and radiation efficiency), enabling the identification of optimal configurations more efficiently[[17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR17 "Sharma A, et al. Characterization and performance enhancement of 4 × 4 microstrip antenna array in dusty atmosphere using metasurface based superstrate. Meas J Int Meas Confed. 2024. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.measurement.2024.114736
                  
                .")]. Techniques like GA [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR18 "Johnson JM, Rahmat-Samii Y. Genetic algorithm optimization and its application to antenna design. Proc IEEE Antennas Propag Soc Int Symp URSI Natl Radio Sci Meet. 1994;1:326–9."),[19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR19 "Ghajar A, Keshtkar A, Jarchi S, Ghorbaninejad H. IEEE Trans Antennas Propag. 2007;55(3):577–82."),[21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR21 "Linden DS, Altshuler EE. Automating wire antenna design using genetic algorithms. Microw J. 1996;39(3):74–81."),[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR22 "Fenlon V, Cooke M, Mayock J, Gallant A, Balocco C. Hybrid algorithm combing genetic algorithm with evolution strategy for antenna design. IEEE Trans Magn. 2015;52(3):1–4.")] and PSO [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR24 "Gajbhiye PA, Singh SP, Sharma MK. Computationally optimized multi-port antenna systems for WLAN/Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11a/h/j/n/ac/ax), 5G (mid-band), and UWB applications. Int J Commun Syst. 2024. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/dac.5985
                  
                ."),[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR25 "Dahbi EK, Elhamadi T, Amar Touhami N. Optimization of the SIW cavity-backed slots antenna for X-band applications using the particle swarm optimization algorithm. J Electromagn Waves Appl. 2022;36(7):928–39."),[26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR26 "Jia X, Lu G. A hybrid Taguchi binary particle swarm optimization for antenna designs. IEEE Antennas Wirel Propag Lett. 2019;18(8):1581–5."),[27](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR27 "Jin N, Rahmat-Samii Y. Parallel particle swarm optimization and finite-difference time-domain (PSO/FDTD) algorithm for multiband and wide-band patch antenna designs. IEEE Trans Antennas Propag. 2005;53(11):3459–68.")] are highly effective at tuning multiple parameters in parallel, improving attributes like gain and minimizing issues such as mutual coupling. PSO, for example, optimizes the spatial positioning and orientation of antennas in antenna arrays, enhancing isolation and signal quality. DNNs excel at identifying complex patterns in data, predicting antenna behavior under different configurations, and reducing the need for extensive empirical testing. RL is useful in dynamic environments, where antennas must adapt to fluctuating conditions like user demand or interference, continuously adjusting configurations for optimal performance. AI/ML models also reduce computational costs by learning from previous designs, making informed predictions and minimizing the need for full-scale simulations, which is particularly advantageous in large-scale antenna design, where traditional simulation methods are computationally expensive.

To gain a clearer understanding, it helps to differentiate between AI and ML. While closely related within computer science, AI is a broader concept encompassing a machine's capability to carry out tasks that typically require human intelligence, such as speech and image recognition, as well as decision-making. ML, on the other hand, is a part of AI and deals with creating algorithms that allow machines to improve their performance using data. These algorithms are trained using large data sets to identify patterns and make predictions or decisions without direct programming [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR11 "Zaidi AM, Singh SP. A dual-band rat-race coupler for high band ratio wireless applications. IEEE Trans Instrum Meas. 2021. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIM.2021.3119131
                  
                ."), [28](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44291-025-00084-9#ref-CR28 "Lay-Ekuakille A, Chiffi C, Celesti A, Rahman MZU, Singh SP. Infrared monitoring of oxygenation process generated by robotic verticalization in bedridden people. IEEE Sens J. 2021. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/JSEN.2021.3068670
                  
                .")]. In short, AI aims to create machines that can perform tasks like humans, while ML is a method for achieving this through machine learning from data. enriched_points.point_title: Use of Machine Learning for Optimization
enriched_points.expanded_content: Machine learning enhances antenna design by predicting behavior, reducing simulation time, and improving efficiency through data-driven optimization, leading to superior performance in complex environments.

Reconfigurable surfaces allow dynamic adaptation of antenna characteristics, enabling functionalities like beam steering and multi-band operation, crucial for meeting evolving communication standards in 5G/6G.
Utilizing advanced materials, such as metamaterials, enhances antenna performance by improving gain, bandwidth, and efficiency, addressing the high demands of modern wireless communication systems.
